label: Disaster Types
definition: SakunaGraPH Disaster Type Classification Scheme based on the Emergency Events Database (EM-DAT) classification.

children.label: Natural
children.definition: Naturally occurring disastrous events.

children.label: Biological
children.definition: A hazard caused by exposure to living organisms and/or their toxic substances (e.g., venom, or mold) or vector-borne diseases that they may carry.

children.label: Animal Accident
children.definition: Human encounters with dangerous or exotic animals in both urban and rural environments.

children.label: Epidemic
children.definition: Either an unusual, often sudden, increase in the number of cases of an infectious disease that already existed in the region or the appearance of an infectious disease previously absent from the region.

children.label: Bacterial Disease
children.definition: An unusual increase in the number of cases caused by exposure to bacteria either through skin contact, ingestion, or inhalation. Examples include salmonella, MRSA, cholera, pertussis.

children.label: Fungal Disease
children.definition: An unusual increase in the number of cases caused by exposure to fungi either through skin contact, ingestion, or inhalation of spores. Examples are fungal pneumonia, fungal meningitis.

children.label: Infectious Disease (General)
children.definition: Either an unusual, often sudden, increase in the number of cases or outbreak of an infectious disease that already existed in the region or the appearance of an infectious disease previously absent from the region.

children.label: Parasitic Disease
children.definition: An unusual increase in the number of cases caused by exposure to a parasite. Exposure occurs mostly through contaminated water, food, or contact with insects, animals, etc. Examples: malaria, Chagas disease, giardiasis.

children.label: Prion Disease
children.definition: A type of biological hazard caused by prion proteins. Prion diseases (TSEs) are rare progressive neurodegenerative disorders affecting both humans and animals. Examples: BSE, Creutzfeldt-Jakob Disease, Kuru.

children.label: Viral Disease
children.definition: An unusual increase in the number of cases caused by exposure to virus through skin contact, ingestion, or inhalation. Common viral illnesses include colds, the flu, COVID-19, norovirus, HPV, and herpes simplex virus.

children.label: Infestation
children.definition: The pervasive influx, swarming and/or hatching of insects, worms, or other animals affecting humans, animals, crops, and perishable goods.

children.label: Grasshopper Infestation
children.definition: Infestation of grasshoppers (insects).

children.label: Infestation (General)
children.definition: General infestation incidents.

children.label: Locust Infestation
children.definition: Infestation of locusts (insects).

children.label: Worms Infestation
children.definition: Infestation of worms.

children.label: Climatological
children.definition: A hazard caused by long-lived, meso- to macro-scale atmospheric processes ranging from intra-seasonal to multi-decadal climate variability.

children.label: Drought
children.definition: An extended period of unusually low precipitation that produces a shortage of water for people, animals, and plants. Drought develops slowly, sometimes over years, and its onset is difficult to detect.

children.label: Glacial
children.definition: Occurs when water held back by a glacier or moraine is suddenly released. Glacial lakes can be at the front of the glacier (marginal lake) or below the ice sheet (sub-glacial lake).

children.label: Wildfire
children.definition: Any uncontrolled and non-prescribed combustion or burning of plants in a natural setting such as a forest, grassland, brush land or tundra. Can be triggered by lightning or human actions.

children.label: Forest Fire
children.definition: A type of wildfire in a wooded area or forest. Can be due to El Niño.

children.label: Land Fire
children.definition: A type of wildfire in a brush, bush, pasture, grassland, or other treeless natural environment. Can be due to El Niño.

children.label: Wildfire (General)
children.definition: Any type of fire in a natural setting such as a grassland, mountain, brush land or tundra, which consumes natural fuels and spreads based on environmental conditions. Can be due to El Niño.

children.label: Extra-terrestrial
children.definition: A hazard caused by asteroids, meteoroids, and comets as they pass near to the Earth, enter the Earth's atmosphere, and/or strike the Earth, and by changes in interplanetary conditions.

children.label: Space Impact
children.definition: A type of extra-terrestrial hazard caused by the collision of a meteoroid, asteroid, or comet with the Earth.

children.label: Space Weather
children.definition: A general term for extra-terrestrial weather conditions driven by solar eruptions such as geomagnetic storms, radio disturbances, and solar energetic particles.

children.label: Geophysical
children.definition: A hazard originating from solid earth. This term is used interchangeably with the term geological hazard.

children.label: Earthquake
children.definition: Sudden movement of a block of the Earth's crust along a geological fault and associated ground shaking.

children.label: Ground Movement
children.definition: Surface displacement of earthen materials due to ground shaking triggered by earthquakes or volcanic eruptions. Often described with magnitudes, intensities, and epicenters.

children.label: Tsunami
children.definition: A series of waves generated by a displacement of massive amounts of water through underwater earthquakes, volcanic eruptions, or landslides. Waves travel at very high speed across the ocean.

children.label: Mass Movement (Dry)
children.definition: Any type of downslope movement of earth materials under hydrological dry conditions.

children.label: Avalanche (Dry)
children.definition: A large mass of loosened earth material, snow, or ice that slides, flows, or falls rapidly down a mountainside under the force of gravity. Under dry conditions.

children.label: Landslide (Dry)
children.definition: Any kind of moderate to rapid soil movement induced by an earthquake under dry conditions. Movement is downward and outward. Often due to earthquakes, affecting houses.

children.label: Rockfall (Dry)
children.definition: A quantity of rock that has fallen from a cliff face under dry conditions. Also called rock slide under dry conditions. Can be due to earthquakes.

children.label: Sudden Subsidence (Dry)
children.definition: Sinking of the ground due to groundwater removal, mining, dissolution of limestone, or earthquakes. Sinking occurs under dry conditions as a result of a geophysical trigger.

children.label: Volcanic Activity
children.definition: A type of volcanic event near an opening/vent in the Earth's surface including volcanic eruptions of lava, ash, hot vapor, gas, and pyroclastic material.

children.label: Ashfall
children.definition: Fine unconsolidated volcanic debris blown into the atmosphere during an eruption; can remain airborne for long periods and travel considerable distances from the source.

children.label: Lahar
children.definition: Hot or cold mixture of earthen material flowing down the slope of a volcano either during or between volcanic eruptions.

children.label: Lava Flow
children.definition: The ejected magma that moves as a liquid mass downslope from a volcano during an eruption.

children.label: Pyroclastic Flow
children.definition: Extremely hot gases, ash, and other materials (>1,000°C) that rapidly flow down the flank of a volcano (>700 km/h) during an eruption.

children.label: Volcanic Activity (General)
children.definition: A type of volcanic event near an opening/vent in the Earth's surface including volcanic eruptions of lava, ash, hot vapor, gas, and pyroclastic material. Explosive eruptions in volcanoes.

children.label: Hydrological
children.definition: A hazard caused by the occurrence, movement, and distribution of surface and subsurface freshwater and saltwater.

children.label: Flood
children.definition: A general term for the overflow of water from a stream channel onto normally dry land in the floodplain, higher-than-normal levels along the coast, and in lakes or reservoirs.

children.label: Coastal Flood
children.definition: Flood along the coast caused by tidal changes or storms. Can be due to tropical storms, cyclones, depressions, heavy rain, or low pressure areas.

children.label: Flash Flood
children.definition: Heavy or excessive rainfall in a short period of time that produces immediate runoff, creating flooding conditions within minutes or a few hours. Can be due to tropical storms, cyclones, heavy rain.

children.label: Flood (General)
children.definition: A general term for flooded areas or floodwaters and the overflow of water levels. Can involve overflow of culverts, knee/waist-deep water levels, sheet flooding. Can be due to heavy rains, monsoons, tropical cyclones.

children.label: Ice Jam Flood
children.definition: The accumulation of floating ice restricting or blocking a river's flow and drainage. Ice jams tend to develop near river bends and obstructions.

children.label: Riverine Flood
children.definition: A type of flooding resulting from the river (swollen river) or overflowing of a stream, creek, or channel onto normally dry land in the floodplain adjacent to the channel.

children.label: Mass Movement (Wet)
children.definition: Types of mass movement that occur when heavy rain or rapid snow/ice melt send large amounts of vegetation, mud, or rock down a slope driven by gravitational forces.

children.label: Avalanche (Wet)
children.definition: A large mass of loosened earth material, snow, or ice that slides, flows, or falls rapidly down a mountainside. Snow Avalanche: Rapid downslope movement of a mix of snow and ice.

children.label: Landslide (Wet)
children.definition: Any kind of moderate to rapid soil movement induced by rain under wet conditions. Can be due to tropical cyclones, storms, depressions. Movement is downward and outward, often affecting houses.

children.label: Mudslide
children.definition: A form of mass wasting involving fast-moving flow of debris and dirt that has become liquified by the addition of water. Also called a mudflow. Can be due to tropical storms, cyclones, heavy rain.

children.label: Rockfall (Wet)
children.definition: A quantity of rock or boulders that has fallen from a cliff face under wet conditions or rain. Can be due to tropical cyclones, tropical storms, tropical depressions, shear line, monsoons.

children.label: Sudden Subsidence (Wet)
children.definition: Sinking of the ground like liquefaction near rivers or seas due to groundwater removal, mining, or dissolution of limestone. Sinking occurs under wet conditions as a result of a hydrological trigger.

children.label: Wave Action
children.definition: Wind-generated surface big waves that can occur near coastal areas or on the surface of any open body of water. The size of the wave depends on the strength of the wind and the distance traveled.

children.label: Rogue Wave
children.definition: An unusual single crest of an ocean wave far out at sea that is much higher and/or steeper than other waves in the prevailing swell system.

children.label: Seiche
children.definition: A standing wave of water in a large semi- or fully-enclosed body of water (lakes or bays) created by strong winds and/or a large barometric pressure gradient.

children.label: Wave Action (General)
children.definition: Wind-generated surface big waves that can occur near coastal areas or on the surface of any open body of water such as oceans, rivers, or lakes.

children.label: Meteorological
children.definition: A hazard caused by short-lived, micro- to meso-scale extreme weather and atmospheric conditions that last from minutes to days.

children.label: Extreme Temperature
children.definition: A general term for temperature variations above (extreme heat) or below (extreme cold) normal conditions.

children.label: Cold Wave
children.definition: A period of abnormally cold weather lasting two or more days, possibly aggravated by high winds. The exact temperature criteria for what constitutes a cold wave may vary by location.

children.label: Heat Wave
children.definition: A period of abnormally hot and/or unusually humid weather lasting two or more days. The exact temperature criteria may vary by location.

children.label: Severe Winter Conditions
children.definition: Damage caused by snow and ice. Winter damage refers to damage to buildings, infrastructure, and traffic inflicted by snow and ice in the form of snow pressure, freezing rain, frozen waterways, etc.

children.label: Fog
children.definition: Water droplets that are suspended in the air near the Earth's surface. Fog is simply a cloud that is in contact with the ground.

children.label: Storm
children.definition: A broad category of severe atmospheric disturbances including cyclones, thunderstorms, blizzards, and other violent weather events.

children.label: Blizzard Storm
children.definition: A low-pressure system in winter months with significant accumulations of snow, freezing rain, sleet, or ice. Winds exceeding 35 mph for three or more hours, producing reduced visibility.

children.label: Derecho
children.definition: Windstorms associated with a convection system. Derechos include downburst and straight-line winds.

children.label: Extratropical Storm
children.definition: A type of cyclonic system in the middle and high latitudes that primarily gets its energy from horizontal temperature contrasts (fronts) in the atmosphere.

children.label: Hail
children.definition: Solid precipitation in the form of irregular pellets or balls of ice more than 5 mm in diameter.

children.label: Sand Storm
children.definition: Strong winds carrying particles of sand aloft, generally confined to less than 50 feet, especially common in arid and semi-arid environments.

children.label: Severe Weather
children.definition: Severe weather, heavy rains and strong winds bringing damage.

children.label: Storm (General)
children.definition: Can be storms brought by monsoons (southwest monsoons) and shear lines.

children.label: Storm Surge
children.definition: An abnormal surge or rise in sea level. Can be due to tropical storms, tropical cyclones, tropical depressions, heavy rain, or low pressure areas.

children.label: Thunderstorms
children.definition: Lightning produced by a thunderstorm and followed by the sound of thunder.

children.label: Tornado
children.definition: Tornadoes and whirlwinds. A violently rotating column of air that reaches the ground or open water (waterspout).

children.label: Tropical Cyclone
children.definition: Can be called typhoons, super typhoons, tropical storms, tropical depressions, and low pressure areas. Characterized by a warm-core, non-frontal synoptic-scale cyclone with a low-pressure center, spiral rain bands and strong winds.

children.label: Technological
children.definition: Man-made events causing impact and technological incidents. Examples: armed conflict, chemical spill, or industrial fires.

children.label: Industrial Accident
children.definition: Technological accidents of an industrial nature/involving industrial buildings (e.g. factories).

children.label: Chemical Spill
children.definition: Accident release occurring during the production, transportation, or handling of hazardous chemical substances.

children.label: Collapse (Industrial)
children.definition: Accident involving the fall or collapse of a building or structure. Can involve fallen industrial or commercial structures like billboards, towers, stores. Can be due to typhoons.

children.label: Explosion (Industrial)
children.definition: Explosions involving buildings or structures of an industrial nature.

children.label: Fire (Industrial)
children.definition: Fire incidents involving industrial buildings or structures.

children.label: Gas Leak
children.definition: Gas leak incidents.

children.label: Industrial Accident (General)
children.definition: Any form of general industrial accidents.

children.label: Oil Spill
children.definition: Accident release occurring during the production, transportation, or handling of oil.

children.label: Poisoning
children.definition: Poisoning of atmosphere or water courses due to industrial sources of contamination.

children.label: Radiation
children.definition: Exposure to harmful radiation or reactive substances.

children.label: Miscellaneous Accident
children.definition: Technological accidents of a non-industrial or transport nature (e.g., involving houses).

children.label: Collapse (Misc)
children.definition: Collapse of a building or structure. Can involve fallen debris, fallen trees, uprooted trees, toppled posts, and soil collapse. Can be due to typhoons, strong winds, tropical storms.

children.label: Explosion (Misc)
children.definition: Non-industrial explosions involving buildings or structures. Grenade/bomb explosions. Can be due to elections and holidays.

children.label: Fire (Misc)
children.definition: Non-industrial fire incidents. Can involve residential structures.

children.label: Miscellaneous Accident (General)
children.definition: Can involve fireworks, drowning incidents, and food poisoning. Does not include transport or vehicular accidents.

children.label: Transport
children.definition: Technological transport accidents involving mechanized modes of transport. Comprises four disaster subtypes: Air, Water, Rail, and Road.

children.label: Air
children.definition: Transport accidents involving airplanes, helicopters, airships, and balloons.

children.label: Rail
children.definition: Transport accidents involving trains.

children.label: Road
children.definition: Transport or vehicular accidents involving motor vehicles on roads and tracks. Can be due to heavy rainfall, earthquakes, or certain special events like holidays. Can involve road cuts and collisions.

children.label: Water
children.definition: Transport or maritime accidents or incidents involving sailing boats (motorbancas), ferries, cruise ships, and other vessels. Can be due to weather conditions, strong winds, typhoons, tropical storms, cyclones.

children.label: Armed Conflict
children.definition: Ambush/shooting, grenade/bombing incidents, can also involve civil disturbances, violence, and gun fires or armed scandals. Often involves victims, killings, and results to deaths or injury.
